perturbation.original_text: Western Copper acknowledges and agrees that a breach of its covenants contained in this Agreement would result in damage to Glamis that could not adequately be compensated for by monetary award alone, Accordingly, Western Copper agrees that in the event of any such breach, in addition to any other remedies available to Glamis at law or otherwise, Glamis will be entitled, as a matter of right and without the need to prove damage, to apply to a court of competent jurisdiction for relief by way of injunction, restraining order, decree or otherwise as may be appropriate to ensure compliance by Western Copper with the provisions of this Agreement and to restrain any breach of this Agreement by Western Copper, Any remedy expressly set forth in this §3.1 wall be in addition to and not inclusive of or dependent upon the exercise of any other remedy available to Glamis at law or otherwise.
perturbation.changed_text: Western Copper acknowledges and agrees that a breach of its covenants contained in this Agreement would result in damage to Glamis. Accordingly, Western Copper agrees that in the event of any such breach, in addition to any other remedies available to Glamis at law or otherwise, Glamis will be entitled to apply to a court of competent jurisdiction for relief. Any remedy expressly set forth in this §3.1 wall be in addition to and not inclusive of or dependent upon the exercise of any other remedy available to Glamis at law or otherwise.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the clause stating damages could not be adequately compensated by monetary award alone, and removing 'as a matter of right and without the need to prove damage' weakens Glamis's ability to obtain injunctive relief, potentially contradicting laws that favor immediate injunctive relief in cases of non-competition agreement breaches. Removing the specific reference to 'injunction, restraining order, decree or otherwise as may be appropriate to ensure compliance' also weakens Glamis's potential remedies.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Availability of Injunctive Relief for Breach of Contract
perturbation.law_citation: B.C. Law and Equity Act, [RSBC 1996] CHAPTER 253, s. 39
perturbation.law_explanation: By removing the explicit acknowledgement that monetary damages may be inadequate and the 'matter of right' to injunctive relief, the modified text introduces ambiguity and potentially contradicts British Columbia law which allows for specific performance or injunctive relief where it is just, even if damages are ascertainable. The original text strengthens the case for injunctive relief by acknowledging the unique nature of the harm, while the modified text weakens it, increasing legal risk for Glamis.

perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly states that monetary damages may be inadequate and grants Glamis the right to injunctive relief without needing to prove damage. The changed text removes these provisions, weakening Glamis's ability to obtain injunctive relief. Snippet 1, referencing the Law and Equity Act [RSBC 1996] CHAPTER 253, includes section 39: 'Injunction or mandamus may be granted or receiver appointed by interlocutory order.' While this snippet doesn't explicitly mandate injunctive relief, the removal of language solidifying Glamis's right to it, combined with the acknowledgement that monetary damages may be inadequate, introduces ambiguity and weakens their legal position. Because the original text strengthens the case for injunctive relief and the modified text weakens it, a contradiction is created.

perturbation.original_text: Right of First Offer 2.2 Glamis covenants and agrees with Western Copper that if at any time it intends to dispose of a Designated Mineral Property for cash consideration or by abandonment, it will give Western Copper notice (the ' Disposition Notice") of the intended disposition. For a period of 30 days from the time of delivery of the Disposition Notice Glamis will, if requested by Western Silver, entertain an offer from Western Copper to acquire the Designated Mineral Property. In the ease of a Designated Mineral Property that Glamis intends to dispose of for cash consideration, the parties will negotiate in good faith to reach a mutually agreeable agreement for the sale to Western Copper of the [Designated Mineral Property. If Glamis and Western Copper are unable to negotiate an acceptable agreement with respect to the Designated Mineral Property within the 30 day period, Glamis may thereafter dispose of the Designated Mineral Property as it sees til in its absolute discretion. If Glamis does not dispose of the Designated Mineral Property within a period of 3 months from the first to occur of the date that Glamis and Western Copper acknowledge failure to negotiate an acceptable agreement with respect to the Designated Mineral Property and the end of the 30 day period, the provisions of this section 2.2 will once again apply to any intended disposition of the Designated Mineral Property by Glamis. In the case of a Designated Mineral Property that Glamis intends to abandon, Glamis wall, if requested by Western Copper, transfer such Designated Mineral Property to Western Copper at no cost save and except for the reasonable costs of transfer incurred by Glamis.
perturbation.changed_text: Right of First Offer 2.2 Glamis covenants and agrees with Western Copper that if at any time it intends to dispose of a Designated Mineral Property for cash consideration or by abandonment, it will give Western Copper notice (the ' Disposition Notice") of the intended disposition. For a period of 30 days from the time of delivery of the Disposition Notice Glamis will, if requested by Western Silver, entertain an offer from Western Copper to acquire the Designated Mineral Property. In the ease of a Designated Mineral Property that Glamis intends to dispose of for cash consideration, the parties will negotiate. If Glamis and Western Copper are unable to negotiate an acceptable agreement with respect to the Designated Mineral Property, Glamis may thereafter dispose of the Designated Mineral Property as it sees til in its absolute discretion. In the case of a Designated Mineral Property that Glamis intends to abandon, Glamis wall, if requested by Western Copper, transfer such Designated Mineral Property to Western Copper at no cost save and except for the reasonable costs of transfer incurred by Glamis.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the explicit requirement to 'negotiate in good faith' when disposing of a mineral property for cash consideration weakens Western Copper's right of first offer. This omission introduces a legal risk because it potentially contradicts the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing present in all contracts under British Columbia law.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Duty of Good Faith in Contractual Performance
perturbation.law_explanation: The omission of the 'good faith' negotiation requirement contradicts the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing, as established in Bhasin v Hrynew. By removing this obligation, Glamis could potentially engage in superficial negotiations or refuse to genuinely consider Western Copper's offers, undermining the intended benefit of the right of first offer and creating a cause of action for breach of contract based on a violation of the implied duty of good faith.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: Holidays In The Workplace - Employee Rights/ Labour Relations - Canada ARTICLE 11 December 2014 Holidays In The Workplace R Rudner Law More Contributor Rudner Law Explore Firm Details In my latest Canadian HR Law Blog post, I discuss the myriad of issues that can arise in the workplace during the upcoming holiday season. Canada Employment and HR Stuart Rudner To print this article, all you need is to be registered or login on Mondaq.com. In my latest Canadian HR Law Blog post, I discuss the myriad of issues that can arise in the workplace during the upcoming holiday season. These include discrimination, drinking and driving, harassment, among other concerns. Generally speaking, holidays and holiday parties can be an opportunity to improve worker morale, or they can have the opposite effect if not handled properly, while also exposing an employer to liability. Thanksgiving is now over (at least in Canada), and Halloween is rapidly approaching. We all know what that means: soon, shopping malls and department stores will start to play holiday music, office holiday parties will be scheduled, and I will continue a relatively new holiday tradition: posting about holidays in the workplace and the issues that this season brings. Two years ago, I wrote Avoiding the risks of holiday parties and Merry Christmas, happy holidays and happy Hanukkah . And then last year, I wrote How to ensure your holiday party doesn't make the news . On a related issue, I also posted the following in December 2012: Do you force employees to use vacation days between Christmas and New Year's? The winter holiday season can be a joyful time for everyone, regardless of your personal background or beliefs. Getting together with colleagues to celebrate, whatever you happen to celebrate personally, can be a way to promote a positive work environment and allow people to get to know those that they work with on a more informal basis. In other words, it can be quite positive. However, there are many risks and dangers that employers should not ignore. This is a bit of a highlight reel from previous p
perturbation.justification: The original contract explicitly required 'good faith' negotiation, whereas the changed contract omits this requirement, potentially allowing Glamis to undermine Western Copper's right of first offer. The Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation both highlight that this omission introduces a legal risk, particularly concerning the implied duty of good faith and fair dealing. Although the snippets provided are not helpful, the removal of this explicit obligation weakens the protections afforded to Western Copper, thus the edited contract creates a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Governing Law and Attornment 4.8 This Agreement will be governed exclusively by and construed in accordance with the laws of the Province of British Columbia, and the parties attorn to the exclusive jurisdiction of the Courts of British Columbia.
perturbation.changed_text: Governing Law and Attornment 4.8 This Agreement will be governed exclusively by and construed in accordance with the laws of the Province of British Columbia.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the phrase 'and the parties attorn to the exclusive jurisdiction of the Courts of British Columbia' creates uncertainty about the jurisdiction where disputes will be resolved. This could potentially contradict legal principles regarding jurisdiction in British Columbia, particularly if one party is not physically present or conducting business there.
perturbation.law_explanation: The removal of attornment to the jurisdiction of the Courts of British Columbia creates a potential conflict with Rule 13-1 of the BC Rules of Court, which deals with service outside British Columbia and the jurisdiction of BC courts over parties not physically present in the province. While the agreement specifies BC law, removing the attornment clause opens the door for arguments that another jurisdiction is more appropriate, potentially leading to increased legal costs and delays, especially if one party resides outside BC. This also affects the CRT's handling of legal disputes.

perturbation.justification: The original text explicitly states that the parties 'attorn to the exclusive jurisdiction of the Courts of British Columbia.' The changed text removes this explicit agreement, potentially contradicting legal principles regarding jurisdiction. While the agreement still specifies BC law, removing the attornment clause opens the door for arguments about jurisdiction. The scraped law snippet provides a link to BC Laws, suggesting the importance of adhering to BC's legal framework, but does not contain any explicit support that a contradiction exists. Based on the perturbation definition, Omission Legal Contradiction arises when a contract removes essential information and in this case, the removal of attornment creates legal loopholes, impacting dispute resolution and potentially making the contract non-compliant.
